role_summary: First full-time executive director of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (1960-1964); chief of staff to Martin Luther King Jr.; architect of the 1963 Birmingham Project C campaign; senior pastor of Canaan Baptist Church in Harlem 1967-2004.
ai_reading: Walker sits at +0.87 on the Nonviolence as Theology vs Armed Self-Defense axis and at +0.73 on the Sacred Framing vs Secular Framing axis, both far toward the poles opposite to his cultural placement. For Martin Luther King Jr.'s chief of staff (1960-1964), the SCLC's first full-time executive director, the architect of the 1963 Birmingham nonviolent direct-action campaign, and a Baptist pastor for thirty-seven years in Harlem, the cultural reading would predict strong placement toward Nonviolence-as-Theology and toward Sacred Framing. The embedding pulls his testimony in the OPPOSITE direction on both axes. His top semantic neighbors confirm the SCLC cultural placement (C. T. Vivian at entry 134 count 128, Dorothy Foreman Cotton entry 29, Reverend Harry Blake entry 102, the Abernathy family entry 28, Timothy Jenkins entry 120), so the cluster pattern is not what's anomalous; the anomaly is in the axis-projection values. This is the second-largest AI-vs-culture mismatch the catalog has surfaced, after the five BPP-identified interviewees clustering at Nonviolence-as-Theology. The two findings together open the question of whether the axis-vector definitions reverse the political mapping in some systematic way, or whether the embedding is catching pragmatic-strategic and institutional-secular language the cultural reception of these figures has filtered out.
biographical_paragraph: Walker served as the first full-time executive director of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference from 1960 to 1964, and as chief of staff to Martin Luther King Jr. across the campaigns of those years [src: 2]. His most consequential strategic contribution was the planning of the SCLC's 1963 Birmingham campaign, known internally as Project C; Walker meticulously surveyed target lunch counters, calculated walking distances from the 16th Street Baptist Church headquarters to downtown sites, and identified federal buildings as secondary protest targets [src: 2]. From 1967 he served as senior pastor of Canaan Baptist Church in Harlem for thirty-seven years; he co-founded the Religious Action Network against apartheid, chaired the Central Harlem Local Development Corporation, and in 1999 founded the Sisulu-Walker Charter School [src: 2]. He died January 23, 2018. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 132 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
